passages.explanation: Jesus crosses back over and comes to his own city. They bring him a paralysed man lying on a bed. Seeing their faith, Jesus tells the paralytic: son, be of good cheer — your sins are forgiven you. Some scribes think to themselves: this man blasphemes. Jesus, knowing their thoughts, asks: which is easier to say — your sins are forgiven, or get up and walk? But so that you may know the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — he tells the paralytic — get up, pick up your bed, and go home. The man gets up and goes. The crowds are filled with awe and glorify God. The healing is the visible proof of the invisible claim. Anyone can say your sins are forgiven — only God can prove it by making a paralytic walk.

Jesus sees a man named Matthew sitting at the tax collection booth and says: follow me. He gets up and follows. While Jesus is eating at Matthew's house, many tax collectors and sinners come and sit with Jesus and his disciples. The Pharisees ask the disciples: why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners? Jesus hears and replies: those who are healthy don't need a physician, but those who are sick. Go and learn what this means — I desire mercy and not sacrifice. I came not to call the righteous, but sinners. Tax collectors were despised as traitors collaborating with Rome. Jesus doesn't just tolerate them — he eats with them, the most intimate social act in that culture.

John's disciples come and ask: why do we and the Pharisees fast often, but your disciples don't fast? Jesus answers: can the friends of the bridegroom mourn while the bridegroom is with them? The days will come when the bridegroom is taken away — then they'll fast. No one puts a piece of unshrunk cloth on an old garment — the patch tears away and the hole gets worse. No one puts new wine into old wineskins — the skins burst, the wine spills, and the skins are ruined. New wine goes into fresh wineskins, and both are preserved. Jesus isn't abolishing fasting — he's saying the old categories can't contain what's happening. The kingdom he's bringing requires new structures.

A ruler comes and kneels before Jesus: my daughter has just died. Come and lay your hand on her, and she will live. Jesus gets up and follows, and so do his disciples. On the way, a woman who has suffered from bleeding for twelve years comes up behind him and touches the fringe of his garment, thinking: if I just touch his clothing, I'll be healed. Jesus turns and sees her: daughter, be of good cheer — your faith has made you well. She's healed from that hour. When Jesus arrives at the ruler's house and sees the flute players and the crowd making a commotion, he says: go away — the girl isn't dead but sleeping. They laugh at him. He goes in, takes her by the hand, and the girl gets up. The news spreads through the whole region. Two desperate people — one a powerful ruler, one an outcast woman. Both approach Jesus with nothing but need. Both receive what they came for.

As Jesus passes on, two blind men follow him, crying out: have mercy on us, son of David! When he comes into the house, the blind men come to him. Jesus asks: do you believe I'm able to do this? They say: yes, Lord. He touches their eyes and says: according to your faith be it done to you. Their eyes are opened. Jesus sternly warns them: see that no one knows about this. But they go out and spread the news about him through the whole land. The title 'son of David' is messianic — these blind men see who Jesus is more clearly than the sighted.

As they leave, a mute man who is demon-possessed is brought to Jesus. When the demon is cast out, the mute man speaks. The crowds marvel: nothing like this has ever been seen in Israel. But the Pharisees say: he casts out demons by the prince of demons. Same miracle, opposite conclusions. The Pharisees can't deny what's happening — so they attribute it to the worst possible source.

Jesus goes about all the cities and villages, teaching in their synagogues, preaching the Good News of the Kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness. When he sees the multitudes, he's moved with compassion because they're harassed and scattered, like sheep without a shepherd. He tells his disciples: the harvest is plentiful, but the labourers are few. Pray that the Lord of the harvest will send out labourers into his harvest. Jesus' response to overwhelming need isn't burnout — it's a call for more workers. The solution to the size of the task is multiplication, not heroism.
